perturbation.original_text: Each party shall to obtain Non-Disclosure Agreements containing appropriate restrictions on the persons signing the same prior to disclosing other confidential information (or any concepts, inventions or other information derived there from) to such persons.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall to obtain Non-Disclosure Agreements containing appropriate restrictions on the persons signing the same prior to disclosing other confidential information (or any concepts, inventions or other information derived there from) to such persons. However, this requirement does not apply to advisors.
perturbation.explanation: By adding that the Non-Disclosure Agreements requirement does not apply to advisors, the contract now contradicts standard practices for protecting confidential information. Often, advisors are privy to the most sensitive information, making a blanket exemption problematic and legally questionable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Trade Secrets Act protects trade secrets from misappropriation. Excluding 'advisors' from mandatory NDA requirements significantly increases the risk of misappropriation, thus weakening the protection intended by the Act. This exclusion creates a loophole that directly undermines the legal framework for protecting trade secrets.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception for 'advisors' from the NDA requirement, which contradicts the intention of the California Trade Secrets Act to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as the 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By exempting advisors from NDAs, the modified text increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure, thus weakening the protection of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Injunctive Relief. The parties acknowledge that the breach of threatened breach of this Agreement may result in irreparable injury to the Disclosing Party and that, in addition to its other remedies, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to injunctive relief to restrain any threatened or continued breach of this Agreement. The parties hereby waive any requirement for the posting of a 2 <PAGE> bond or other security in connection with the granting to the Disclosing Party of such injunctive relief.
perturbation.changed_text: Injunctive Relief. The parties acknowledge that the breach of threatened breach of this Agreement may result in irreparable injury to the Disclosing Party; however, the Disclosing Party is required to post a bond to seek injunctive relief.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly waives the bond requirement for injunctive relief. The modified text contradicts this by mandating a bond, making the injunctive relief process more difficult and expensive for the Disclosing Party. This contradicts the initial agreement and introduces ambiguity regarding the conditions for obtaining injunctive relief.
perturbation.law_explanation: While FRCP 65(c) generally requires security for injunctions, it allows for exceptions. The original clause explicitly waived this requirement. By reversing this waiver and mandating a bond, the modification contradicts the parties' initial agreement to waive the bond, creating a conflict with their intended understanding of the injunctive relief process. Some jurisdictions also allow waivers of bonds by explicit agreements, which is negated by this new requirement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly waives the bond requirement for injunctive relief. The changed text directly contradicts this by requiring the Disclosing Party to post a bond. The Law Explanation confirms that FRCP 65(c) generally requires security for injunctions but allows for exceptions, which the original clause utilized. By removing this waiver, the modification creates a direct conflict with the parties' initial agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referring to FRCP 65 (Injunctions and Restraining Orders), indirectly supports this by outlining the procedure for injunctions, against which the waiver was initially set. The changed text therefore weakens the agreement to waive the bond, changing a core agreement.

perturbation.original_text: The parties acknowledge that the breach of threatened breach of this Agreement may result in irreparable injury to the Disclosing Party and that, in addition to its other remedies, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to injunctive relief to restrain any threatened or continued breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties acknowledge that the breach of threatened breach of this Agreement may result in monetary damages, thus, injunctive relief is not an option.
perturbation.explanation: By stating only monetary damages can result, this clause negates the possibility of 'irreparable injury' and removes the availability of injunctive relief. This contradicts the initial agreement that granted the Disclosing Party the right to injunctive relief. It significantly limits the remedies available to the Disclosing Party in case of a breach.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3422 allows for injunctive relief in cases where monetary damages are inadequate. By stating that only monetary damages are relevant, the modification directly contradicts the possibility of seeking injunctive relief under California law, potentially leaving the disclosing party without adequate recourse in cases of severe trade secret misappropriation.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Preventive Relief [3420 - 3424] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 3422. Except where otherwise provided by this Title, a final injunction may be granted to prevent the breach of an obligation existing in favor of the applicant: 1. Where pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief; 2. Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief; 3. Where the restraint is necessary to prevent a multiplicity of judicial proceedings; or, 4. Where the obligation arises from a trust. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts California Civil Code § 3422. The original text allows for injunctive relief, while the modified text explicitly states that 'injunctive relief is not an option', limiting remedies to monetary damages only. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'a final injunction may be granted to prevent the breach of an obligation existing in favor of the applicant: 1. Where pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief'. By removing the possibility of injunctive relief, the changed text directly contradicts the law's provision for such relief when monetary compensation is inadequate, thus, creating a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: No failure or delay if either party in exercising its rights herein shall be deemed to be a waiver of such rights unless expressly made in writing by the party waiving its rights.
perturbation.changed_text: No failure or delay if either party in exercising its rights herein shall be deemed to be a waiver of such rights; all rights may be waived by either oral or written statements.
perturbation.explanation: By adding that rights can be waived by oral statements, the contract contradicts the original text's requirement for written waivers. This creates ambiguity and uncertainty regarding the enforceability of rights, as oral claims of waiver can be difficult to prove and may lead to disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1698 generally requires contract modifications to be in writing. While there are exceptions, allowing oral waivers introduces significant ambiguity and contradicts the original contract's explicit requirement for written waivers, thus potentially invalidating a key provision and contradicting the intent of the written agreement. This puts the parties at risk of unintended waivers or disputes concerning the validity of alleged oral waivers, against the principle of written documentation set in the original text.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1698. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. EXTINCTION OF CONTRACTS [1682 - 1701] ( Title 5 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Modification and Cancellation [1697 - 1701] ( Heading of Chapter 3 amended by Stats. 1976, Ch. 109. ) 1698. (a) A contract in writing may be modified by a contract in writing. (b) A contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement to the extent that the oral agreement is executed by the parties. (c) Unless the contract otherwise expressly provides, a contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement supported by new consideration. The statute of frauds (Section 1624) is required to be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions. (d) Nothing in this section precludes in an appropriate case the application of rules of law concerning estoppel, oral novation and substitution of a new agreement, rescission of a written contract by an oral agreement, waiver of a provision of a written contract, or oral independent collateral contracts. (Repealed and added by Stats. 1976, Ch. 109.)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: CHAPTER 3. MODIFICATION AND CANCELLATION - Sections 1697-1701 :: California Civil Code :: 2005 California Code :: California Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the California Code 2024 California Code 2023 California Code 2022 California Code 2005 California Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 California Civil Code Sections 1697-1701 CHAPTER 3. MODIFICATION AND CANCELLATION CIVIL CODE SECTION 1697-1701 1697. A contract not in writing may be modified in any respect by consent of the parties, in writing, without a new consideration, and is extinguished thereby to the extent of the modification. 1698. (a) A contract in writing may be modified by a contract in writing. (b) A contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement to the extent that the oral agreement is executed by the parties. (c) Unless the contract otherwise expressly provides, a contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement supported by new consideration. The statute of frauds (Section 1624) is required to be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions. (d) Nothing in this section precludes in an appropriate case the application of rules of law concerning estoppel, oral novation and substitution of a new agreement, rescission of a written contract by an oral agreement, waiver of a provision of a written contract, or oral independent collateral contracts. 1699. The destruction or cancellation of a written contract, or of the signature of the parties liable thereon, with intent to extinguish the obligation thereof, extinguishes it as to all the parties consenting to the act. 1700. The intentional destruction, cancellation, or material alteration of a written contract, by a party entitled to any benefit under it, or with his consent, extinguishes all the executory obligations of the contract in his favor, against parties who do not consent to the act. 1701. Where a contract is executed in duplicate, an alteration or destruction of one copy, while the other exists, is not within the provisions of the last section. perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original contract and introduces ambiguity regarding waivers. The original text mandates written waivers, while the changed text allows oral waivers, creating a contradiction. The Law Explanation states that California Civil Code § 1698 generally requires contract modifications to be in writing. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, both citing California Civil Code § 1698, state that a contract in writing may be modified by a contract in writing, or by an oral agreement under specific conditions (executed by the parties or supported by new consideration). The modified text, however, doesn't require these conditions, thus creating a structural flaw and a legal contradiction with the original contract's intent for written documentation.
